records.summary: Product-specific pressures distinguish authored source provenance from framework-fixed catalog facts and warn against false field-level edit precision.
records.rationale: The user explicitly noticed AI-generated provenance records that did not look thought through, especially framework property provenance. This shard makes that pressure queryable without rereading the entire product-specific pressure document.
guidance: Use product.architecture field-provenance rows before deciding that a fact needs field-level provenance.
Authored TS/HTML/configuration facts may need exact field spans; framework-fixed catalog facts often need product/source provenance instead.
If a provenance fallback appears, trace the lower-level identity threading before accepting defensive source broadening.

anchors.heading: Provenance And Evidence
anchors.summary: Product obligation notes for provenance and evidence.

records.summary: Context economics is an inquiry-algebra pressure: callers should pay for the minimum honest substrate needed for the product question.
records.rationale: Multiple long runs found that slow phases can mean either ordinary product-feature cost or missing low-level indexes. This shard preserves the distinction so future performance work does not collapse back into generic caching or smoke tests.
guidance: Profile deep enough to separate repeated primitive costs from product-feature costs.
When a product question does not need binding observation, TypeChecker projection, router activation, or full app-world construction, expose that as inquiry algebra.
For mixed monorepos and external roots, run workspace/app-shape lanes before expensive semantic-runtime opening.

anchors.heading: Context Economics
anchors.summary: Product obligation notes for multi-resolution answers and cost boundaries.

records.summary: Agent handoff is the compact post-orient index of Atlas commands and when to use each lane.
records.rationale: Future sessions should not rediscover the available Atlas handles by scanning package scripts or old workbench prose. This shard makes the handoff document findable from memory queries.
guidance: Run orient first, then memory, then the pressure/profile lane that matches the current domain.
Prefer Atlas lanes over au-mcp or ad hoc repo mapping.
When a lane feels too expensive or too noisy, improve Atlas rather than storing a manual workaround in markdown.

records.summary: MCP is a late stable public API surface; Atlas remains the internal self-improving development map.
records.rationale: The operator corrected an earlier suggestion to put legacy-replacement and internal development utility into MCP. MCP requires restarts and is meant for end users, so it cannot be the autonomous-loop workbench. Internal corpus, legacy, and product-pressure lenses belong in Atlas until the semantic-runtime public API is stable enough to expose.
guidance: Do not expose Atlas workbench lenses or framework corpus lenses directly as MCP resources.
Use Atlas for internal self-improving inquiry because it can be changed and exercised during autonomous loops.
Treat MCP package work as a packaging step over stable user-facing semantic-runtime concepts, not as a substrate-discovery tool.
When mining docs/tests, record the internal Atlas/product pressure separately from eventual public MCP shape.

anchors.heading: MCP Packaging
anchors.summary: MCP packaging pressure and public API framing.

records.summary: Framework corpus docs/tests pressure now has an internal Atlas lens with expected-effect contracts, fixture-seed rows, and `ts.source` continuations for non-Program docs/tests ranges.
records.rationale: The north-star review pack and user steering both point at Aurelia docs/tests as pressure inputs for authoring taste and behavior grounding, but not as direct semantic truth or MCP API shape. The `framework.corpus` lens classifies docs, doc code fences, framework test files, test call snippets, source-backed semantic-runtime expected-effect contract rows, and fixture seeds joined to those expected-effect descriptors so future Codex can inspect patterns without rereading large markdown/test corpora or inventing a parallel effect vocabulary. `ts.source` can now read exact file/range/position/token text from source files outside the TypeScript Program, and corpus row pages expose source-inspection continuations, while checker-backed projections remain Program-only.
guidance: Use `framework.corpus` projections before opening the raw docs or framework tests: docs/doc-snippets for promoted app-building pattern and tests/test-snippets for behavior grounding.

Use `expected-effects` to inspect the source-backed semantic-runtime expected-effect contract, including effect-kind rows, effect-role rows such as `baseline`, `signature`, and `discriminator`, and effect-kind seed policy; filter with `effectKind`, `effectRole`, or `effectSeedPolicy` when needed.

Treat `project-tooling` as an app-shell reopen baseline in corpus pressure, not a docs/tests corpus-pattern seed; generated source plans own package manifest, TypeScript config, and declaration file pressure.

Use `fixture-seeds` with `effectKind`, `appPatternKey`, `expectedEffectFilterField`, `expectedEffectFilterValue`, `classificationKind`, and `classificationKey` filters when choosing the next fixture/app-builder pressure target. Seed classifiers read the exact snippet source range, not the display preview; exact `surface:*` reasons such as `surface:native-value-binding`, `surface:native-checked-binding`, `surface:option-model-binding`, and `surface:validation-binding-behavior` are local to doc code fences and test `createFixture(...)` call snippets, while parent `describe`/`it` carriers remain effect, app-pattern, or contrast pressure. CLI filters may use `classificationKind=surface` plus the bare key, or the printed `surface:*` reason in `classificationKey`, which Atlas normalizes. Use default all-token `query` mode for exact-ish corpus narrowing, and `queryMode=partial` only for exploratory multi-term sweeps over docs/tests where adjacent examples are more useful than a zero-row answer. Form-service-state app-pattern hints require an explicit state-owned service surface: form + DI + service alone is direct-service pressure and should route as `pressure-fixture`, while form + DI + service + concrete state/store surface may seed service-owned form app-pattern pressure.

Aurelia interpolation and bare `else` should be classified contextually: HTML/markup snippets can count `${...}` and `else` as template pressure, while ordinary TypeScript template strings and JavaScript `else` branches should not. A `forms` concept routes form-related fixture pressure, but binding value-channel/data-flow effects require binding or value-channel syntax rather than form service code alone. Likewise, router fixture seeds require concrete router authoring/runtime syntax such as `@aurelia/router`, `@route`, route config objects, or `au-viewport`; broad route/router prose remains corpus navigation pressure only.
Documentation under testing guides is behavior-grounding rather than promoted app-promoted app-building pattern, even when the fenced block contains real router or fixture examples.

Binding-behavior fixture seeds are source-text classified from actual `& behavior` syntax; `& validate` and validate-binding-behavior prose may also hint `form-validation-surface`, while the `binding-behavior-application` effect requires an application-shaped snippet. When a seed has exactly one behavior name or static literal argument, the expected-effect row carries compact filters such as `behaviorName=validate` or `staticArgumentValues=blur`.

Validation is its own corpus concept rather than a sub-bucket of broad forms. Use `framework:corpus -- --projection=fixture-seeds --concept=validation --seedUse=authoring-taste` when looking for validation docs pressure such as result presentation, `validation-errors`, `validation-container`, or validate binding-behavior examples.

Binding target/value/data-flow fixture seeds carry `targetProperty` filters only when source text has a single value-channel binding target such as `value.bind`, `checked.two-way`, or `model.bind`; listener commands such as `click.trigger` and `submit.trigger` stay unfiltered because they are event surfaces, not value channels. Multi-target snippets intentionally stay unfiltered rather than implying a conjunction the source did not mean.

Style fixture seeds should classify stylesheet/style-binding snippets separately: stylesheet/style registry snippets attach the `style-resource` effect, direct class/style/css renderer or `.class`/`.style` binding surfaces attach `target-operation` only when found inside HTML-like tag spans, and binding value-channel effects attach only when source text contains class/style/value-channel syntax such as class/style interpolation, `.class`, `.style`, or value/model/checked binding commands. Whole class/style bindings and per-token/per-property bindings carry different channel filters; `.class` value-channel/data-flow filters use the class token as `targetProperty`, while `.style` value-channel/data-flow filters use the concrete CSS property and target-access filters stay on `style`.

AuCompose fixture seeds are source-surface classified from concrete `<au-compose>` markup or `AuCompose` API usage. They carry the `runtime-composition` expected-effect hint, `surface:au-compose`, and `appPatternKey=dynamic-composition-surface` so dynamic composition work can start from structured filters instead of broad text search. Narrow surface keys cover component/model/template inputs, scope/flush/tag literals, composition/composing outputs, and object-shaped component values. In semantic-runtime, dynamic AuCompose bindings and static literal bindables enter through different lanes: controller-bind target accesses for `.bind` inputs and hydrate-instruction `SetPropertyInstruction`s for literal `scope-behavior`, `tag`, and `flush-mode`.

Use `seedUse=authoring-taste` or `seedUse=behavior-grounding` to choose docs/tests as taste pressure or behavior pressure; `authoring-taste` expected effects are orientation contracts and should not be expected to have direct corpus fixture rows.

Use `query` for source/content concepts after structural filters; fixture-seed relevance ranks path, preview, concepts, and effect hints without letting app-pattern names such as `form-state-surface` satisfy every form query. Broad `minimal-app-surface` app-pattern hints should stay out of snippets that already carry more specific form, DI, state, validation, or router pressure. Use `expectedEffectFilterField` plus `expectedEffectFilterValue` instead of query text when you need a concrete structured fact such as `staticArgumentValues=blur` or `targetProperty=value`.

Filtered fixture-seed ranking is intentionally focus-aware: when a caller asks for a concept such as `forms`, source paths/groups that actually belong to that concept outrank broad snippets from router or error-message docs, but rows without semantic effect hints are demoted below effect-bearing fixture seeds. If broad corpus output looks surprising, inspect classification reasons before changing app-builder or fixture policy.
Follow fixture seed source ranges with `ts.source`/`projection=text` when a snippet needs context; filesystem-backed source text should report source-text basis only, not TypeScript Program basis.

Fixture seeds already join effect hints to the semantic-runtime expected-effect contract; seedable corpus-pattern descriptor gaps are useful pressure, while reopen-baseline/orientation/closure contracts without snippets are expected. Deepen next by linking snippets to more exact framework source concepts rather than adding unbounded text dumps.
Keep docs frequency separate from idiom policy; docs are a pressure signal that must be checked against framework source and product semantics.
Use framework corpus pressure for authoring/taste work and framework test corpus pressure for behavior grounding.
Do not make these corpus lenses public MCP surfaces.
